themes: MCPS school rating system overhaul | Listener asked April 30 for a deep dive on the new state rating system replacing the one-to-four-star model — work has begun but no launch date is published.

Andrew Friedson's actual fiscal record | Listener has now asked twice (April 27, April 29) whether Friedson really is the fiscal-discipline candidate or just framed that way; needs roll-call evidence, not reputation.

Wootton High School relocation | Listener has asked April 29 (and earlier) why MCPS wants to close and move Wootton; episodes 4/30 ep1 and 5/01 ep2 covered process but the full timeline of the Crown decision is still owed.

Property-tax-vs-MCPS funding trade-off | Listener has flagged property tax as too high (April 27) and supports cutting non-essential spending (April 28); maps directly to the Friedson/Elrich budget feud.

Historical voting record for filed candidates | Listener restated April 26 that the show is shallow on candidate history; for filed names (Friedson, Glass, Jawando, Kramer, Crutchfield, Stewart, Tichy, Cox, Bouchat, Hale, Moore) we owe career-long sourcing not just last-session votes.
open_questions: What does the new MCPS school rating system actually measure, and how would it have changed the rating of schools in cluster 005 (Wootton)?
Is Andrew Friedson actually the fiscal-restriction candidate — what is his roll-call record on county property-tax increases in 2020, 2023, 2025, 2026?
Why did MCPS choose Crown over a Wootton renovation, and what authority would a County Executive have to revisit that decision?
What is Kramer's full historical voting record on education funding — every annual budget vote with the count?
Side-by-side policy comparison of the three Democratic County Executive candidates (Friedson / Jawando / Glass) on tax, housing, immigration, school funding, and policing.
underserved_topics: Career-long voting history for each filed candidate (not just current-session activity).
Old news / public record search for District 19 Delegate filers (Crutchfield, Kramer, Stewart, Tichy) — listener has had names but no biographies for two weeks.
Concrete cost-side breakdown of MCPS supplemental requests vs the county's own tax-rate ceiling (the math the property-tax skeptic wants to see).
How the new MCPS rating methodology will categorize Wootton, Whitman, Churchill, and other Council District 1 / District 7 schools.
Republican gubernatorial primary mechanics — listener has the names (Cox, Bouchat, Hale) but no policy comparison, even though they are not voting in it.
summary: This listener is a first-time Democratic primary voter in Rockville (Montgomery County, MD-19, Council District 7) seven weeks out from the June 23 primary. They want concrete, candidate-linked voting records — bill numbers, dates, roll-call counts — not vibes. Their fixed priors are visible: skepticism toward property-tax increases, opposition to MCPS spending hikes when they show up on the tax bill, support for non-essential spending cuts, deep skepticism of Kramer's all-yes pattern on education funding, and a now-resolved demand to know which Democratic candidates oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration. The newest ask is a deep dive on the new MCPS school rating system.
avoid_list: The framing "a listener asked us last week/earlier this week which Democratic primary candidates actually oppose sanctuary policy on illegal immigration" used as a setup line — appears verbatim or near-verbatim in both 2026-04-28 ep1 and 2026-04-29 ep3

The Evan Glass bill requiring background screening for DHS agents joining Montgomery County police — introduced and framed as the concrete sanctuary-policy example in 2026-04-28 ep1 and again in 2026-04-29 ep3
The ICE agent mask-ban ordinance passed by the Montgomery County Council — covered as the sanctuary-policy benchmark in 2026-04-29 ep2
"Two stories, properly worked through" / "two stories, not spreading thin across six" as an episode-framing device — used in 2026-04-29 ep1
Governor Moore's "Leave No One Behind" slogan contrasted with disability services cuts — used in 2026-04-27 ep1 and ep1.rewrite
The ACA marketplace quiet-downgrade story ("thousands of Marylanders have been quietly downgrading their health insurance plans") — introduced in 2026-04-28 ep1 and revisited in 2026-04-29 ep3

Maryland's 90-day part-time legislature explanation ("they meet in Annapolis for ninety days, the rest of the time they're lawyers, teachers, small business owners") — used in 2026-04-26 ep3 and ep3.rewrite
The wave of 25 Maryland lawmakers retiring or running for other offices framing — used in 2026-04-26 ep1, ep1.rewrite, and ep4
The Beltsville Agricultural Research Center closure story as a District 19 candidate-record hook — used in 2026-04-27 ep4 and ep4.rewrite
Listener's own frustration about promised candidate deep-dives not delivering — narrated back across at least five episodes; do not repeat as a framing device again

The Friedson April 28 letter calling for permanent police in every MoCo high school sent two days before the Blake High shooting — covered in 2026-04-30 ep1 and 2026-05-01 ep1; do not re-tell as "spooky timing" again

The Maryland Voting Rights Act of 2026 vs Louisiana v. Callais Supreme Court timing — explained from scratch in 2026-04-30 ep2, 2026-05-01 ep1 and ep3; do not re-explain the federal Section 2 backstory
JORDAN saying "we did not actually answer Wootton on Wednesday" / "we do not just sound different, we vote different" as the episode-opening accountability beat — used in 2026-05-01 ep1
The Comptroller Brooke Lierman healthcare-workforce 427,000-jobs report — covered in 2026-05-01 ep3; do not re-cite the headline number

Direct enumeration of the listener's assigned district numbers (LD-19, MD-8, Council District 7, Precinct 08-008, etc.) used as a credibility flex inside the script — these are configuration, not content
